planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant rooted divisions in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; space 6–9 inches apart and mulch well the first year to maintain moisture and suppress weeds until the plants fill in.

directions: Root stem cuttings or set out plugs in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade, spacing 6–9 inches apart; Japanese spurge is the classic deep-shade groundcover for the eastern United States, forming a dense, weed-suppressing mat of glossy, dark-green, whorled leaves that are evergreen year-round and tolerate the challenging dry shade under mature trees. White flower spikes appear in mid-spring, followed by the new growth flush. Requires acidic soil (pH 4.5–6.0) — plants yellowing in alkaline soil respond to acidifying fertilizer and sulfur applications. Once established it spreads steadily by underground stems and requires minimal care. Susceptible to pachysandra blight (Volutella) in humid climates; improve air circulation and avoid overhead irrigation.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring or fall; sweet box spreads slowly by suckers to form a dense colony over many years.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; sweet box is a refined, slow-growing, suckering Asian shrub with small, polished, lance-shaped leaves and inconspicuous tiny white flowers in late winter to early spring that produce one of the most intensely sweet vanilla-honey fragrances in the plant world — remarkable for their power from such small, unshowy blooms. Small, glossy black berries follow in late summer. The plant is entirely shade-adapted and makes an excellent low-maintenance, deer-resistant groundcover or fragrant foundation planting under trees where little else succeeds. 'Purple Stem' (Sarcococca hookeriana var. humilis) stays more compact at 18 inches. No significant pruning required.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall or container plants in early spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil at pond margins, stream banks, or in consistently wet areas in partial shade to full sun.

directions: Plant divisions in early fall or set out container plants in early spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil at pond margins, stream banks, or boggy depressions in partial shade to full sun; marsh marigold is among the first wildflowers to bloom each spring, producing brilliant buttercup-yellow flowers on hollow stems above glossy, kidney-shaped leaves in March-April before many trees have leafed out. It thrives in standing water or saturated soil and colonizes bog garden edges and stream margins naturally. After bloom in early summer, the plant goes dormant in hot weather; pair with later-emerging water plants like Iris pseudacorus to fill the gap. All parts are toxic when raw — handle with gloves. Divide in late summer every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or plant divisions in early spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; cuckooflower naturalizes aggressively in damp meadows and is ideal for wet, shaded naturalistic plantings.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant divisions in early spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to dappled light; cuckooflower (lady's smock) produces delicate, lilac-pink four-petaled flowers on slender stems in mid-spring above rosettes of pinnate, watercress-flavored leaves. It is a native of European wet meadows and stream edges and one of the most important larval host plants for orange-tip butterflies. The young leaves have a pleasant peppery, watercress-like flavor and are edible in salads. Naturalizes readily by self-seeding in consistently moist, partly shaded conditions. The double-flowered form 'Flore Pleno' is a long-lived, non-seeding selection that spreads only by offshoots.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in well-drained to moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; greater wood rush is one of the most tolerant groundcovers for deep shade, dry shade, and acid soil under conifers.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained to moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; greater wood rush forms spreading rosettes of broad, glossy, grass-like leaves with a distinctive fringe of white hairs along the margins, making it one of the most versatile shade groundcovers and one of the very few plants that tolerates the extreme dry shade and acidic conditions under large established conifers. Brown flower spikelets appear in early spring on stems above the foliage. The gold-variegated cultivar 'Aurea' provides year-round bright-gold foliage color even in deep shade and is especially effective along woodland paths. Spreads steadily by stolons; divide every 4–5 years to renew vigor and control spread.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring or fall; plant at least one male per several females for berry production.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; skimmia is a refined Japanese shrub with glossy, aromatic, evergreen leaves that provides three seasons of interest — fragrant white flower clusters in spring, persistent bright-red berries (on female plants) that remain decorative through winter, and handsome foliage year-round. It is one of the finest shade shrubs for dense-shade situations that defeat most flowering shrubs. The plant is dioecious — you need at least one male (often sold as Skimmia japonica 'Rubella' or 'Fragrans') for every three female plants to produce berries. Yellowing leaves indicate iron or manganese deficiency in alkaline soil; treat with chelated iron. Never allow plants to dry out — they wilt dramatically in drought.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; maidenhair fern grows slowly but is long-lived in appropriate conditions with consistent moisture.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; northern maidenhair fern is one of the most graceful ferns in cultivation, with distinctive, horseshoe-shaped fronds of fan-shaped, bright-green leaflets carried on delicate, wiry, shining black stems that tremble in the slightest breeze. New growth in spring emerges as coppery-pink croziers (fiddleheads) before expanding to full size. It requires consistently moist soil and fails in dry, compacted, or alkaline conditions. Excellent in combination with trilliums, Solomon's seal, and other woodland perennials. Divide in early spring by lifting and cutting the rhizome mass — do not allow divisions to dry out before replanting. The tropical maidenhairs (A. raddianum and relatives) require completely different culture.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; royal fern is an ideal choice for pond margins, stream banks, and boggy areas where it may eventually reach 6 feet in height.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun at pond margins, stream banks, and bog garden edges; royal fern is the most massive and architecturally bold of the temperate ferns, eventually forming vase-shaped clumps 4–6 feet tall with large, compound fronds that look more like a tropical plant than a typical fern. The fertile fronds bear distinctive brown, tassel-like spore clusters at their tips from late spring through summer. The unfurling cinnamon-brown croziers (fiddleheads) in spring are edible when young and delicate. The plant is long-lived, building over many decades into a substantial clump with a fibrous rootball of accumulated peaty root material. The closely related cinnamon fern (O. cinnamomea) has very similar requirements.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
